title: Nine Days Later
synopsis: Tracks how war-driven disruption in the Strait of Hormuz exposed the petrodollar loop as physically fragile, accelerating global moves toward alternative energy and payment systems.
preview: On February 19th, 2026, I published an essay arguing that the convergence of artificial intelligence, robotics, and solar energy would eventually d...
content:
On February 19th, 2026, I published an essay arguing that the convergence of artificial intelligence, robotics, and solar energy would eventually displace the petrodollar system. I expected the transition to take years. Possibly decades. I was careful about timing. I said, more than once, that anyone who claimed to know the schedule was lying or selling something.

Nine days later, the United States and Israel launched a joint military operation against Iran.

Within four days the Strait of Hormuz was functionally closed, a fifth of the world's oil supply was stranded, and the physical mechanism through which the US dollar maintains global reserve status was exposed, publicly and undeniably, as fragile in exactly the way the essay described. I am not claiming the petrodollar system has ended. I am claiming something more specific: that this war has made visible, to every government and every market participant on Earth, the structural vulnerability at the centre of the current monetary order. And that visibility changes everything, regardless of how the war itself resolves.

To understand why, you need to see the machine. Then you need to watch it break.

The petrodollar is not an abstraction. It is a loop, and the loop has physical geography.

Oil flows from the Gulf states through the Strait of Hormuz, a passage thirty-three kilometres wide at its narrowest point. It flows to Asia, to Europe, to every economy that runs on combustion. The Gulf states sell that oil in US dollars. Those dollars do not sit idle. They recycle: into American Treasury bonds, into US equities, into the financial architecture that allows the United States to run persistent deficits without consequence. Investments from the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Kuwait into American financial markets have been growing rapidly for over a decade. Gulf sovereign wealth is deeply embedded in equities, including, notably, the handful of AI and technology companies that account for a disproportionate share of stock market growth.

The recycled dollars create global demand for the currency, which sustains its reserve status, which funds the military that protects the Gulf states, which keeps the oil flowing through Hormuz. Oil to dollars to Treasuries to military to Gulf to oil. A circuit. Self-reinforcing. Stable, so long as every element holds.

Every loop has a point of maximum fragility. A place where, if you cut, the whole circuit fails. For the petrodollar, that point has always been the Strait. Strategists have war-gamed Hormuz closures for decades. The scenario appears in think-tank reports with the regularity of weather forecasts. But there is a difference between knowing a thing intellectually and watching it happen on a flight-tracking website while tanker rates hit all-time records and European gas prices climb seventy percent in a week.

On February 28th, someone cut the loop.

The operation was called Epic Fury. In the early hours of the morning in Tehran, Israeli and American forces launched a decapitation strike against Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the Supreme Leader of Iran. He was killed, along with members of his family and several senior officials. The stated American objective, delivered by President Trump in a video address, was effectively regime change. Cut off the head and the body falls.

The doctrine has a logic to it. The logic is wrong, but it has a shape that looks persuasive on a briefing slide. It assumes a centralised enemy. An enemy whose command structure is hierarchical, whose population is brittle, whose will to fight depends on the survival of a single leader. Iran is not that enemy.

The Shia tradition, which shapes Iranian political and religious identity, has martyrdom at its centre. The death of Khamenei did not decapitate the state. It galvanised it. He was eighty-six. He had cancer. He could have sheltered in Moscow. State media framed his remaining in Tehran as a chosen sacrifice, a call to jihad that eighty million people received as a binding obligation. And the decentralised command structure Iran has spent decades building meant that every region had its own orders, every cell had its own targets. The head was removed. The body kept moving. The mismatch between the doctrine and the reality is the first fracture.

Iran's response was not directed at Washington. It was directed at the machine.

Missiles and drones struck across the Gulf Cooperation Council states. Dubai. Abu Dhabi. Bahrain. Qatar. Kuwait. Not random targets. Three categories, chosen with the precision of an engineer dismantling a system rather than a soldier fighting a battle. American military bases: the security guarantee that makes Gulf commerce possible. Energy infrastructure: the oil fields and processing facilities that generate the petrodollars. And, held in reserve but credibly threatened, the desalination plants that produce sixty percent of the region's fresh water, without which these desert cities become uninhabitable within weeks.

You need to understand what the GCC actually is to understand why this works. These are cities in deserts where no city should exist. Eighty percent of food imported. Fresh water manufactured. Populations that are, in Dubai's case, ninety percent expatriate. The entire arrangement functions because petrodollars flow in and goods and services flow back. It is an artificial system sustained by a single commodity denominated in a single currency, protected by a single military. Attack all three simultaneously and the structure does not bend. It seizes.

Dubai International Airport, the busiest in the world for international passengers, went dark. Over twelve thousand flights were cancelled across the region. Hotel cancellation rates hit eighty percent. More than a million passengers were stranded. Wealthy expatriates reportedly offered a quarter of a million dollars for a single departing seat. Amazon data centres in the UAE and Bahrain lost power. Emirates, the world's largest long-haul airline, suspended all operations indefinitely. Dubai officials have estimated that an unplanned airport shutdown costs approximately one million dollars per minute once the cascading effects reach airlines, cargo, tourism, and dependent businesses.

But the economic damage, significant as it is, is not the point. The point is the perception. For decades, Dubai sold itself as Switzerland with sunshine. Neutral. Safe. A place where wealth could park itself without risk. That perception ended in an afternoon. Missiles were intercepted in the skies above the city. The calculus for every expatriate, every corporation, every fund manager considering Gulf exposure, has been permanently altered. Capital flight from the Gulf is not a temporary disruption. It is a structural repricing that no ceasefire can fully reverse.

And then the Strait closed.

Not through a formal naval blockade. The mechanism was subtler and, in some ways, more devastating. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps broadcast on open radio channels that no vessel would be permitted to pass. A handful of tankers near the strait were struck by drones and missiles. A few were set ablaze. That was sufficient.

Ship owners assessed the risk. Insurers withdrew protection and indemnity coverage. Without insurance, no commercial vessel can operate. Within seventy-two hours, traffic through the strait dropped to effectively zero. Over 150 ships anchored outside the passage, waiting. The world's most critical energy corridor shut down not through military force but through actuarial mathematics. The petrodollar's aorta did not need to be severed. It just needed to become uninsurable.

The cost asymmetry deserves attention. Iran's Shahed drones cost approximately fifty thousand dollars each. The American interceptors used to defend against them cost one to three million dollars per missile, and frequently require multiple launches per target. Iran produces an estimated five hundred drones per day. Each one can destroy a tanker, an oil field, or a desalination plant. The defenders spend forty to sixty times what the attackers spend, depleting stockpiles that take months to replenish while the attackers manufacture replacements overnight. This is not a war the United States can win through superior technology. The weapon systems were designed for a different kind of conflict, built to impress rather than to attrite. The asymmetry is structural. It does not improve with time.

Now follow the money.

Oil prices surged more than fourteen percent in the first week. Supertanker rates from the Middle East to China hit an all-time record of $423,000 per day. European natural gas prices climbed over seventy percent after Qatar halted LNG production under drone threat. The S&P 500 dropped over two percent in a single session. South Korea's Kospi tumbled more than five percent. Gold breached $5,400. The dollar strengthened as a haven in the short term, which is what currencies do in a crisis, but the mechanism that generates organic global dollar demand, the petrodollar loop itself, was visibly degrading beneath the surface.

If the disruption persists for weeks, these signals become structural. Oil above one hundred dollars per barrel. Inflation reigniting across every import-dependent economy. The Federal Reserve unable to cut rates at precisely the moment the economy needs stimulus. Gulf sovereign wealth redirected from American equities toward domestic survival. The stock market, which carries a quarter of its weight in a handful of technology companies heavily invested in by Gulf nations, exposed to a withdrawal of the capital flows that have been quietly sustaining it.

The loop does not break all at once. It breaks in sequence. The oil stops. The dollars stop recycling. The security guarantee is revealed as insufficient. The perception of safety collapses. And every participant in the system is forced, for the first time, to calculate what happens if the loop does not resume.

Here is what they are calculating.

Japan. Seventy-five percent of its oil from the Gulf. The Prime Minister has said the economy runs out in eight or nine months if the strait stays closed. India. Over half its LNG imports Gulf-linked, with a significant share Brent-indexed, creating a simultaneous oil and gas price shock. Pakistan and Bangladesh. Ninety-nine and seventy-two percent of their LNG from Qatar and the UAE respectively. South Korea. Net oil imports approaching three percent of GDP.

The short-term response is rationing and reserve drawdowns. The medium-term response is the one that matters: accelerated investment in energy that does not require passage through a thirty-three-kilometre chokepoint controlled by a hostile power. And the long-term response is the one that changes the world order: the construction of trade and payment systems that do not depend on the US dollar.

Both are already further along than most Western commentary appreciates. China installed 240 gigawatts of solar in 2025 and has already peaked its domestic oil demand. Global energy transition investment hit $2.3 trillion. On the financial side, ninety-seven percent of trade among the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation's ten member nations was conducted in local currencies last year. China's cross-border payment system processes trillions of yuan daily, growing at over sixty percent per year. The dollar's share of global reserves has declined from seventy-one percent to just over fifty-six percent since 2008. Central banks have been buying over a thousand metric tonnes of gold annually for three consecutive years. These are not protest gestures. They are functioning systems, waiting for exactly this kind of crisis to prove their necessity.

Every day the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the case for using them strengthens in every finance ministry and central bank in the non-Western world. Not as ideology. As survival.

I do not know how this war ends. The American president has suggested four to five weeks. The strategic logic, on both sides, suggests longer. Iran's decentralised structure makes capitulation unlikely. The American doctrine assumes a centralised enemy that breaks under pressure. Iran is not breaking.

But however the war resolves, the world that emerges from it will not be the world that entered it.

Every monetary transition in the last 150 years has followed the same pattern. Gradual erosion of the underlying physical basis. Then a crisis that makes the erosion undeniable. Then a rapid restructuring that, in retrospect, looked inevitable and in real time looked impossible. The gold standard did not end because economists argued it should. It ended because a war revealed that the system could not accommodate the reality it was supposed to govern. Bretton Woods did not end because of a policy paper. It ended because fiscal pressure made the peg indefensible.

The petrodollar's physical basis is oil, priced in dollars, flowing through the Gulf, protected by the American military. As of the last week of February 2026, every element of that basis is under direct assault. The oil is not flowing. The Gulf is not safe. The military cannot prevent fifty-thousand-dollar drones from closing the world's most important shipping lane. And the alternatives, the solar installations and the currency swap agreements and the payment systems that bypass SWIFT, are not waiting for permission. They already exist. They were built quietly, over years, by nations that understood, perhaps before the rest of us, that the old architecture was already hollow.

The petrodollar does not need to collapse in a single dramatic event. It needs enough participants to quietly complete their migration to alternatives, so that when the next crisis arrives, and the next crisis always arrives, the old loop tries to function and discovers that the room is empty.

Whether this is that crisis, or merely the rehearsal for it, depends on variables I cannot predict. The duration of the conflict. The resilience of Gulf infrastructure. The pace of Asian energy diversification. The political will of nations that have the resources to build the next system but have not yet decided to act.

I think the window is shorter than most people believe. The pattern suggests these things happen faster than consensus expects. But the honest thing to say is that timing remains the weakest part of the thesis.

What is not weak is the direction. The loop is breaking. The alternatives are built. And nine days after I wrote that the transition was coming, the war that may complete it began.

The pattern does not wait for permission. It runs on its own schedule, indifferent to whether we are ready.
